chapter_summary: This record covers two textual fragments bundled together. (1) Bhagavad Gita Chapter 1 (Arjuna-Vishada Yoga), verses 1-15: On the field of Kurukshetra (dharmakshetra), the blind king Dhritarashtra asks his charioteer Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas, gathered with intent to fight, did. Sanjaya reports that Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army arrayed in battle formation by Drupada's son (Dhrishtadyumna), approaches his teacher Drona and enumerates the great warriors on the Pandava side (Bhima, Arjuna, Yuyudhana, Virata, Drupada, Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, Kashiraja, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, Shaibya, Yudhamanyu, Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra, and the sons of Draupadi) and on his own side (Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, the son of Somadatta). He calls his own force (guarded by Bhishma) insufficient and the enemy's (guarded by Bhima) sufficient, and urges all to protect Bhishma at every entry of the formation. Bhishma, the veteran Kuru grandsire, roars like a lion and blows his conch; then conches, drums and horns sound a tumultuous, frightening din. Krishna (Madhava/Hrishikesha) and Arjuna (Pandava/Dhananjaya), seated in a great chariot drawn by white horses, blow their divine conches: Krishna blows Panchajanya, Arjuna blows Devadatta, and Bhima (Vrikodara) of terrible deeds blows the great conch Paundra. (2) Narada Purana Chapter 1, part 2, verses 22-46: a hymn of surrender (stuti) by Narada to Vishnu as the supreme, unborn, formless yet all-pervading Self, knowable through knowledge (jnana), meditation (dhyana) and yoga; praised as the source and inner Self of all gods (Brahma, Indra, Rudra/Shiva) who appears in countless forms. The hymn enumerates Vishnu's avatars: Kurma (tortoise who bore Mandara at the churning of the ocean of nectar), Varaha (boar who raised the earth from the waters), Narasimha (man-lion who tore the demon Hiranyakashipu and saved Prahlada), Vamana (the unconquered dwarf who measured the worlds in two strides, taking the earth from Bali/Vairochana), Parashurama (Jamadagnya, who twenty-one times severed the kshatriya lineage for Haihaya's offense), Ramachandra (surrounded by monkeys, slayer of the rakshasa army), Krishna (who in a dual form removed the earth's burden and withdrew his own clan), and Kalki (who at the end of the yuga severs the wicked with a sharp blade and re-establishes dharma at the start of the Krita age). The hymn affirms that mere remembrance of the Lord's name liberates (as Gajendra the elephant-king was freed), repeatedly takes refuge in Him, and declares his forms innumerable, beyond counting even in crores of years.

story.title: On Kurukshetra and the Many Forms of the One
story.arc: Two openings of revelation, framed by a question and a hymn. In the first, blind Dhritarashtra asks what happened on the field of dharma; Sanjaya answers by drawing back the curtain on the two armies, naming the warriors, sounding Bhishma's conch and then the divine conches of Krishna, Arjuna and Bhima — the war about to begin. In the second, Narada answers a different kind of question: who is the One behind it all? He surrenders to Vishnu as the formless Self, then unrolls that single being into His descents — tortoise, boar, man-lion, dwarf, axe-bearer, Rama, Krishna, Kalki — proclaiming the forms uncountable. Together: the cosmos in motion (battle) and the cosmos in essence (the avatars of the One).
story.timeline_note: The Gita fragment is the eve of the Kurukshetra war (Mahabharata main timeline). The Narada Purana hymn is non-narrative and atemporal, recalling avatars across all yugas (Satya through the end of Kali, culminating in Kalki at the start of the next Krita age).
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana running to his old teacher to nervously list the enemy's heroes, then insisting his own larger army is the 'insufficient' one — anxiety dressed up as a war briefing. And the cosmic deadpan of Narada cataloguing infinite avatars only to give up: 'cannot be counted even in crores of years.'

teachings.teaching: The battlefield is called dharmakshetra, the 'field of dharma' — the very place of conflict is simultaneously the field where right action is decided.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, Kurukshetra is the body itself and the war is the inner struggle of the sadhaka; the spine/sushumna is the field where contending tendencies (the hundred Kauravas as sense-impulses, the five Pandavas as disciplined faculties) meet, and dharma is decided in the act of inner battle, not on outer ground.

teachings.teaching: The divine conches (Panchajanya, Devadatta, Paundra) sounded by Krishna, Arjuna and Bhima announce the awakening that precedes action.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the conch-sound is the inner anahata nada, the un-struck sound heard in deep Kriya meditation; its blowing by Krishna (the indweller / Hrishikesha, lord of the senses) signals the rousing of consciousness within the practitioner before the inner battle.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme is the formless, unborn, all-pervading Self — knowable not by the senses but through knowledge (jnana), meditation (dhyana) and yoga; He dwells in the heart yet remains unknown to those deluded by maya.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Sharma's Kriya teaching: God is realized within, in the heart (hridi sthitah), through direct meditative knowing rather than ritual or intellect; maya is the veil Kriya practice dissolves so the ever-present, pure Self (shuddha, niranjana) shines forth to the one who has withdrawn from all attachments (sarvasanga-nivritta).

teachings.teaching: The one Supreme Being appears in countless descents (Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Kalki) to bear the world's burden and protect the devotee — the forms are innumerable, beyond counting.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's view the multiplicity of avatars expresses a single consciousness assuming forms as needed; for the yogi each descent is also an inner event — the lifting of the earth (Varaha) and the tearing of the demon to save Prahlada (Narasimha) mirror the raising of awareness out of inertia and the destruction of ego that liberates the devotee absorbed in remembrance.

teachings.teaching: Mere remembrance and chanting of the Lord's name liberates: as Gajendra the elephant-king was freed from the crocodile's fierce grip by calling out, so the devotee is saved.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the Gajendra rescue as the power of constant inner remembrance (japa/ajapa) in Kriya: the 'crocodile' is the grip of worldly bondage, and the saving name is the breath-borne mantra that, sustained, releases the soul without any other effort.

teachings.teaching: Surrender (sharanagati) to the One is repeatedly affirmed as the refuge of the seeker — 'I take refuge in Him' returns as the hymn's refrain.
teachings.lens_note: Surrender in Sharma's Kriya is not passivity but the offering of breath, ego and action to the indwelling Self; the repeated 'tam asmi sharanam gatah' is the attitude that makes meditation fruitful, the dropping of the doer so the ever-pure witness can be realized.
